core_concept: Entrepreneurship and Innovation: How New Ideas Change Markets and Create Economic Growth
facts: Entrepreneurs identify unmet needs in a market and create businesses to meet them, which can lead to new products or services that did not exist before.
Innovation—new products, processes, or business models—drives economic growth by making production more efficient or creating entirely new markets.
A patent gives an inventor the exclusive right to make, use, or sell an invention for a limited time, which encourages innovation by protecting the inventor's investment.
Disruption occurs when a new business model or technology displaces established market leaders, often by offering a simpler or cheaper alternative that meets customers' needs in a new way.
Starting a business involves risk because many new ventures fail; entrepreneurs must be willing to accept that failure is a normal part of the process.
Technology companies like those that created smartphones or online shopping have transformed global economies by changing how people communicate, buy goods, and access information.
misconceptions: Students often think that all entrepreneurs become rich quickly, but in reality most startups fail and even successful ones take years to become profitable.
Students may believe that innovation always means inventing something completely new, but innovation can also be a new process or business model that improves an existing product or service.
Students sometimes think that patents last forever, but patents only protect an invention for a limited time, after which anyone can use the idea.
